docs.title: 🌟 Welcome to Northstar Developer Docs
docs.text:
Northstar is a modern, high-performance Configuration Management Database (CMDB) platform developed by Astrona. It delivers physical asset visualization, 2D CAD blueprint room designers, multi-floor server cabinet layouts, active discovery subnet scanners, and declarative GitOps reconciliation loops.

This documentation conforms strictly to the Diátaxis framework to support logical, goal-oriented technical learning and references.

docs.title: 🗺️ Explore the Documentation Quadrants
docs.text:
Our documentation is separated into four distinct quadrants based on your current task and mode of learning:

🎓 Tutorials (Learning-Oriented)

Guide for newcomers. Follow a step-by-step practical lesson to bootstrap the backend and frontend servers locally.

Get Started → 🍳 How-To Guides (Problem-Oriented)

Short recipe guides to help you solve specific tasks, such as creating custom categories or bulk importing cabling patches.

View Recipes → 📖 Reference (Information-Oriented)

Hard, factual specifications. Check Go database models, GORM schema properties, and REST API payload definitions.

Browse API Specs → 💡 Explanation (Understanding-Oriented)

Deep-dive architecture discussions on the JWT-RBAC policy model, GORM migration ledgers, and GitOps template compilers.

Read Architecture →

docs.title: System Architecture & Technical Deep-Dive
docs.text: This document provides a comprehensive technical overview of Northstar's underlying systems architecture, detailing the JWT-RBAC policy weights model, the YAML GitOps bootstrap engine, and the modular REST router topology.

docs.title: 🏗️ Core Architecture Overview
docs.text:
Northstar is structured as a decoupled, full-stack client-server application optimized for extreme reliability, fast startup cycles, and minimal metadata transaction latency.

graph TD
    User([User Browser]) <--> |Nuxt UI / Vue 3| Frontend[Nuxt 4 Web Server]
    Frontend <--> |Asynchronous REST JSON| Backend[Go Echo REST API]

    subgraph Go Backend
        Backend --> |Modular Routing| Router[internal/router/router.go]
        Router --> |Controllers / GORM Preloads| Handlers[internal/handlers/]
        Handlers --> |Relational Schema Mapping| Models[internal/models/]

        Models <--> |Transactions / SQLite3| DB[(SQLite Database File)]

        DB -.-> |Automatic Versioning| Migrations[internal/database/migrations.go]
        DB -.-> |Declarative Manifests| Seeder[internal/database/bootstrap.go]

        Handlers -.-> |JSON Webhook Alerts| Webhooks[Outbound Broadcaster]

        Router --> |Traces / HTTP Spans| OTel[internal/telemetry/]
        DB --> |Traces / SQL Spans| OTel
    end

    Webhooks -.-> |Signed POST Callbacks| Integrations([Slack / JIRA API])
    OTel -.-> |OTLP over HTTP/JSON| Collector([OpenTelemetry Collector / Jaeger])

docs.title: 🔐 Advanced Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
docs.text:
Northstar enforces a relational, weighted permission-policy model securely parsed over JWT authentication headers. It stands out by resolving hierarchical policy overrides between Access Groups and User Overrides:

  1. Access Group Policy Weights: Users can belong to a Group (such as the Operations Group which allows the asset:write policy).
  2. User Explicit Overrides: Users can be directly linked to individual explicit permissions. If a User has a permission linked with a deny effect (such as catalog:write marked with deny), GORM's policy weighted resolver will automatically prioritize the explicit user denial, overriding any broad group allowance.

The authorization handshake operates statelessly via GORM checks inside the Echo REST middleware:

sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    Client->>Backend: Request (Bearer JWT Token)
    Note over Backend: Middleware extracts claims (Username, Role)
    Backend->>DB: Query User with Group and Permissions Preloaded
    DB-->>Backend: Relational User Struct
    Note over Backend: Resolve policy overrides: explicit denies trump allowances
    alt Allowed
        Backend->>Handlers: Execute Endpoint Controller
        Handlers-->>Client: Success JSON (200 OK)
    else Denied
        Backend-->>Client: Unauthorized Error (403 Forbidden)
    end

docs.title: 🚀 YAML Bootstrapping & GitOps Reconciliation
docs.text:
To support standard DevSecOps practices, Northstar integrates a declarative GitOps Bootstrapping Engine that runs automatically on every server cold start:

  • Directory Auditing: On boot, the seeder walks .northstar-data/bootstrap/ recursively.
  • YAML Ingestion: The seeder parses modular single-resource manifests matching the network.northstar.astrona.io/v1alpha1 Kubernetes API specifications.
  • GORM Reconciliation: Utilizing GORM's FirstOrCreate transaction boundaries, the system reconciles existing categories, sub-groups, users, and groups, updating metadata attributes dynamically without resetting active relational IDs or triggering GORM integrity warnings.

docs.title: 📊 OpenTelemetry Observability Architecture
docs.text:
Northstar is fully instrumented with standard-conforming OpenTelemetry APM Tracing to guarantee complete visibility across complex request flows:

  1. Echo HTTP Ingress Middleware: All incoming REST endpoints are intercepted by the standard OpenTelemetry Echo tracer. This automatically starts a new root Span for the request lifecycle, capturing the HTTP route, method, request payload metadata, and client IP, injecting trace headers cascade-style across downstream outbound API hooks.
  2. GORM Database Callback Hooking (GormOTelPlugin): Northstar features a custom, high-performance database tracing plugin hooked directly into GORM v2 callbacks. When GORM starts a transaction (such as Query, Create, Update, or Delete), the plugin intercepts the operation, extracts the context, starts a child Span representing the SQL database call, captures the exact executed SQL statement (e.g. SELECT * FROM categories), and records GORM error exceptions dynamically.

docs.title: How-To Guides: Custom Fields & Bulk Ingest
docs.text: These guides provide problem-oriented, practical recipes to help you solve specific operational tasks within the Northstar platform.

docs.title: How to Declare a Custom Field Schema with Regex Constraints
Problem
docs.text: You want to enforce a strict formatting constraint (such as requiring IP addresses, MAC addresses, or precise serial numbers) on a dynamic custom attribute added to your assets.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Use the administrative Custom Fields Creator under Site Admin to register the field with a custom compiled regular expression validator.

  1. Navigate to Settings and select the Site Administration tab (GitLab-Style Sidebar).
  2. Click on the Custom Schema Attributes panel.
  3. Fill out the creation form:
    • Field Name: rack_u_index
    • Value Type: number
    • Category Scope: Server
    • Validation Regex Pattern: ^[1-9][0-9]?$ (Restricts values to numbers between 1 and 99)
    • Form Tab Group: Hardware Metrics
  4. Click Deploy Attribute Schema.

The creation wizard will dynamically compile the regex rule, blocking non-compliant inputs inside the wizard on Step 2 with visual validation errors.

docs.title: How to Bulk Ingest Inventory CSV Files
Problem
docs.text: You have hundreds of newly acquired server rack chassis and network routers that you want to ingest into Northstar without registering them individually in the UI.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Upload your CSV asset manifest via the CSV Bulk Ingest Engine, which runs pre-compliance checks before committing anything to GORM.

  1. Ensure your CSV file is formatted correctly with the standard headers: 
    name,type,status,ip_address,description,cpu_model,memory_gb
Dublin-Router-01,Router,active,10.0.5.1,Core backhaul router,Broadcom DNX,64
Dublin-Edge-02,Server,active,10.0.5.2,Edge server cluster,AMD EPYC,128
  2. Navigate to Site Admin → CSV Bulk Ingest Engine.
  3. Under Target Data Type, choose CMDB Assets & Hardware.
  4. Drag and drop your .csv file into the upload zone.
  5. Click Run Pre-Compliance Checks.
  6. Review the inline Verification Report:
    • Green rows represent compliant records.
    • Red rows indicate cells violating active custom fields validation regex rules (e.g. invalid IPs or out-of-bounds metrics).
  7. Resolve any highlights in your local spreadsheet, re-upload, and click Commit Data Ingest Sweep to write records to GORM.

docs.title: How to Bulk Connect Cabling Patches
Problem
docs.text: You want to map the physical copper/fiber cable matrix connecting routers and switch ports inside a 42U rack.

docs.title: Solution
docs.text:
Use the Cabling Matrix upload selector inside the CSV Bulk Ingest panel.

  1. Create a .csv file detailing source and destination nodes: 
    source_asset_name,source_port,target_asset_name,target_port,cable_type,link_speed_gbps
Dublin-Router-01,eth1,Dublin-Switch-02,eth24,fiber,10
Dublin-Router-01,eth2,Dublin-Switch-03,eth24,fiber,10
  2. Upload the file under the Cable Patch Matrix toggle.
  3. The parser will verify that both source and target hardware exist in GORM, automatically linking them in the relational mapping and rendering pulsing cabling tracers on the interactive SVG topology map!

docs.title: How-To: OpenTelemetry Observability (APM Tracing)
docs.text: This guide provides a practical recipe explaining how to spin up a local observability stack (such as Jaeger) and configure the Northstar backend to export live, distributed trace spans.

docs.title: Running a Local Jaeger Observability Stack
docs.text: The easiest way to view traces locally is using Jaeger, an open-source distributed tracing APM platform. Jaeger supports standard OpenTelemetry OTLP ingestion out of the box.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Ensure you have Docker and docker-compose installed on your system.

docs.title: Step 1: Spin up Jaeger via Docker
docs.text:
Run the following command to start a local Jaeger container exposing the OTLP HTTP receiver on port 4318 and the search UI on port 16686:

docker run -d --name jaeger \
  -e COLLECTOR_OTLP_ENABLED=true \
  -p 16686:16686 \
  -p 4317:4317 \
  -p 4318:4318 \
  jaegertracing/all-in-one:latest

Once running, access the Jaeger Search UI at http://localhost:16686.

docs.title: Configuring the Northstar Backend
docs.text:
By default, Northstar attempts to send HTTP/JSON OTLP spans to localhost:4318. If no collector is running, the background OTel engine will gracefully log a message and continue without blocking.

To customize or activate full tracing, configure the following environment variables:

Environment Variable Recommended Value Purpose OTEL_ENABLED true Explicitly enables OpenTelemetry trace exporting (disabled by default to keep local terminals silent). OTEL_SERVICE_NAME northstar-cmdb Identifies this service inside your APM dashboard. OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT localhost:4318 The OTLP HTTP receiver host and port. OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_INSECURE true Allows unencrypted HTTP connections for local testing.

docs.title: Starting the App with Tracing Enabled
docs.text:
If you are using our justfile shortcuts, start the backend with the required environment variables:

OTEL_ENABLED="true" \
OTEL_SERVICE_NAME="northstar-cmdb" \
OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT="localhost:4318" \
OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_INSECURE="true" \
just backend

Now, navigate to the Nuxt frontend at http://localhost:3000, log in, and click around the dashboard or register a new asset.

docs.title: Verifying Traces in Jaeger
docs.text:
1. Open http://localhost:16686 in your browser.
  2. Under the Service dropdown, select northstar-cmdb.
  3. Click Find Traces.
  4. Click on any trace to inspect:
    • HTTP Incoming Spans: View execution times and status codes of REST routes.
    • GORM Database Spans: Drill down to see the exact SQL query strings (such as SELECT * FROM categories) executed against the SQLite database file!

docs.title: REST API Endpoint Reference
docs.text: This reference document outlines the REST API endpoints, payload contracts, authentication, and HTTP response schemas exposed by the Northstar Echo backend on port 8000.

docs.title: Base Configuration & Headers
docs.text:
All REST operations utilize JSON bodies. Endpoints modifying the state (POST, PUT, DELETE) require a JWT bearer authentication token in the request header.

  • Base URL: http://localhost:8000
  • Content-Type: application/json
  • Authorization Header: Bearer <token>

docs.title: POST /api/auth/login
docs.text: Generates a signed JWT session token valid for 24 hours.

docs.title: Request Body
docs.text:
{
  "username": "admin",
  "password": "admin"
}

docs.title: Successful Response (200 OK)
docs.text:
{
  "token": "eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9...",
  "username": "admin",
  "role": "admin"
}

docs.title: GET /api/assets
docs.text: Retrieve a list of registered hardware and cloud-native assets.

docs.title: Query Parameters
docs.text:
• q (string, optional): Searches asset names and descriptions.
  • type (string, optional): Filters by specific asset category (e.g., Server, Database).
  • status (string, optional): Filters by asset operational state (e.g., active, maintenance).

docs.title: Successful Response (200 OK)
docs.text:
[
  {
    "id": "e2f75a6c-b248-4cb2-a7cf-3467bf2ea904",
    "name": "Production DB Server",
    "type": "Database",
    "status": "active",
    "ip_address": "10.0.1.5",
    "description": "Main PostgreSQL database",
    "properties": {
      "engine": "PostgreSQL",
      "version": "14.2"
    },
    "maintenance_status": "none"
  }
]

docs.title: POST /api/assets
docs.text: Creates a new configuration asset in the database. Requires asset:write permission.

docs.title: Request Body
docs.text:
{
  "name": "Edge-Switch-A",
  "type": "Network",
  "status": "active",
  "ip_address": "10.0.1.200",
  "description": "Rack A core switch",
  "properties": {
    "sub_group": "Switch (L3)",
    "ports": 48
  }
}

docs.title: Successful Response (201 Created)
docs.text:
{
  "id": "2a75904c-3ca1-4091-bf99-0d1234901f4c",
  "name": "Edge-Switch-A",
  "type": "Network",
  "status": "active",
  "ip_address": "10.0.1.200",
  "properties": {
    "sub_group": "Switch (L3)",
    "ports": 48
  }
}

docs.title: GET /api/datacenters
docs.text: Retrieves all registered physical location profiles including nested floor blueprint relations.

docs.title: Successful Response (200 OK)
docs.text:
[
  {
    "id": "86a08489-ef65-45b5-814e-90a6900ea458",
    "name": "Northstar-Dublin-HQ",
    "type": "homelab",
    "country": "Ireland",
    "city": "Dublin",
    "properties": {
      "uplink_speed": "2.5 Gbps Fiber WAN"
    },
    "floors": [
      {
        "id": "f2901a4c-bf12-4091-88fc-ef12a45c90f2",
        "datacenter_id": "86a08489-ef65-45b5-814e-90a6900ea458",
        "name": "Floor 1",
        "level": 1,
        "width": 20,
        "depth": 20
      }
    ]
  }
]

docs.title: Getting Started with Northstar
docs.text: This tutorial is a hands-on, learning-oriented introduction to Northstar. You will learn how to bootstrap a clean local workspace, spin up the Go backend, run the Nuxt 3 dashboard, and register your first physical server asset.

docs.title: 🎯 Learning Objectives
docs.text: By the end of this tutorial, you will have: 1. Checked out and set up the correct runtime environments (Go + Node). 2. Bootstrapped a fresh local database seeded with Astrona taxonomy models. 3. Authenticated your user operator session and browsed the 2D CAD canvas. 4. Created and verified a physical server asset linked to a network interface.

docs.title: 🛠️ Prerequisites
docs.text: To complete this tutorial, you need to install the following on your machine: - Golang 1.20+ - Node.js 24+ & npm - Git

docs.title: Step 1: Clone the Codebase and Run Setup
docs.text:
First, clone your repository and navigate to the project root directory.

Run the package setups for both layers of the stack:

=== "Go Backend Setup" 

cd backend
go mod download
cd ..

=== "Nuxt Frontend Setup" 

cd frontend
npm install
cd ..

docs.title: Step 2: Start the Go REST Server
docs.text:
The Go backend handles automatic GORM schema migrations, processes SBOM SPDX uploads, and performs active subnet discovery scans.

Start the backend server: 

cd backend
go run cmd/server/main.go

Verify that you see the following initialization logs in your terminal: 

[Migration Engine] Applying automatic GORM schema AutoMigrate updates...
[Bootstrap Engine] All modular K8s-style bootstrapping manifests reconciled successfully.
Starting Go server on port 8000...

docs.title: Step 3: Run the Nuxt 3 Dashboard
docs.text:
In a separate terminal window, start the frontend development server: 

cd frontend
npm run dev

Once compiled, navigate your web browser to http://localhost:3000. You will be automatically redirected to the secure Sign-In page.

docs.title: Step 4: Sign In and Register Your First Asset
docs.text:
1. Sign In: Use the pre-seeded operator credentials:
  2. Username: admin
  3. Password: admin
  4. Access Wizard: Click the glowing green "Add Asset" button in the top right corner of the dashboard.
  5. Choose Category: Select Server on Step 1.
  6. Enter Attributes:
  7. Name: Northstar-Edge-01
  8. IP Address: 10.0.1.15
  9. CPU Model: Intel Xeon D-1541 (Fill under Custom Properties tab)
  10. Save and Verify: Click Save. The dashboard list will instantly populate with your new asset, displaying an active monitoring card and scheduling hooks.

🎉 Congratulations! You have successfully set up, run, and registered your first physical hardware asset on the Northstar platform!
